posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked:
I was just watching the Somm2: Into the Bottle (which I think is great btw), and noticed a brief shot of an ancient looking walled vineyard around minute 26. No mention of what the vineyard is, but I was intrigued. Wondering if anyone here knows.

IMG-36711920×1440 334 KB

posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked: Chapter 3: “The History”. It’s right at the 26 minute mark.

posts.name: Rohit 13h@ñ
posts.cooked: I could be way off, but I know some of the vineyards in Burgundy (like Montrachet) have stone walls around them.

posts.name: Nate Simon
posts.cooked: Looks like enough room to land a helicopter, just sayin’.

posts.name: P@trick Lynch
posts.cooked:
Can you give some context? Was it part 1, 2, or 3? 1: It doesn’t look like Germany to me. 2: it could be near Rome, though it doesn’t fit my impression of Lazio. 3: N. Rhone has some Clos, and those vines look old. My bet is somewhere in that region.

This is how that chapter was described:
3. The History

Schloss Vollrads (in Rheingau, Germany) has been making wine since 1200 and has produced 800 vintages. Dr. Rowald Hepp may be their winemaker, but he seems aware that he is simply part of the Schloss’s history. He puts on white gloves and delicately opens a volume in the wineries guilted library from 1492…

Then we meet Harvard Professor Steven Shapin, a historian who goes into detail about what wine was like in Roman times, including the so-called “Golden Wine.” Paul Grieco chimes in about how wine was designed to be drunk and enjoyed in ancient times.

We meet Jean-Louis Chave, the winery to know on Hermitage hill in the Northern Rhône (land of French Syrah). He shares with us a very rare bottle of 1969 Ermitage which was made by his grandfather. He is taken aback when he takes his first sip, he seems to understands more about his grandfather.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Looks like Burgundy to me - walled clos, appropriate background landscape, single Guyot pruning, 10k/hectare planting density, trellising posts at around 1m high. As for where exactly I’m a bit hard pressed. Maybe Clos des Perrières of Grivault or something like that? Or maybe somewhere in northern Nuits? That’s quite a long slope in the background that could be consistent with that.

posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked: There’s not much context at all. The shot is towards the beginning of chapter 3, and the voiceover is discussing how vineyards in Europe have been around for a long time, and that they are passed from generation to generation. That’s really it. No description of particular regions or vineyards were specific then “Europe”.
One of the small Clos in Volnay or Pommard?

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
Paul_Bruck:

Pommard

Yes! I was thinking Grand Epenots when I saw this! But it’s such a blind guess…
posts.display_username: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.cooked:
No, the walls are too close to each other for Grands Epenots,
maybe Clos de Verger - or Volnay Clos de la Rougeotte
?

reply_to_user.name: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Safe to say not a biodynamic vineyard

posts.name: Nick Christie
posts.cooked:
To piggyback off of William, the two potential locales that stand out to me (because of Wall consistency & slope) would be somewhere adjacent to Clos des Lambrays, i.e. Northern Nuits, or somewhere Southern with a Chardonnay planting, given how much scorched earth I saw there in late March. Even at Bienvenue Batard…

But I legitimately only have recency bias in this analysis .
